reasoning: Confirmed by direct code inspection. Lines 15-18 of /home/openclaw/Repos/Research/2026/wp-plugins-research/plugins/src/duplicate-page/inc/admin-settings.php use sanitize_text_field(htmlentities($_POST[...])) for all four settings fields. htmlentities() converts characters like &, <, >, " to HTML entities (e.g. & -> &) before sanitize_text_field() runs. sanitize_text_field() does NOT decode HTML entities — it strips tags and normalizes whitespace but leaves entity strings intact. The entity-encoded string is then stored in the DB via update_option(). On retrieval at line 80, esc_attr() is applied to the stored value without first decoding entities, causing double-encoding: a stored & becomes &amp; in the output HTML attribute. An administrator entering a suffix like 'Copy & Paste' would see it rendered as 'Copy &amp; Paste' in the settings input field. Reachability is correctly stated: administrator-only (current_user_can('manage_options') + nonce check at line 12). The correct WP pattern is sanitize_text_field(wp_unslash($_POST[...])) without any htmlentities() call. This is a data integrity defect, not a security exploit, so low severity is appropriate.
